claims.claim: We investigate whether a negative-exit controller—trained to identify and skip candidates unlikely to be correct—can reduce tail latency and expansion count in best-of-N search over language model outputs, while preserving task accuracy.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Using a linear controller trained on real-model search traces from Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct (Q4_K_M quantization) served via llama.cpp, we evaluate on 50 tasks (40 GSM8K-style math, 10 MBPP-style code) with 8 candidates per task.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: At matched 95% task accuracy, the negative-exit controller reduces p95 latency by 0.4034 s (~21.7%) relative to a positive-only baseline, but p95 expansion count remains unchanged at 2.0.

claims.claim: Best-of-N search over language model outputs is a common strategy for improving task accuracy: generate N candidates, evaluate each, and select the best.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The cost of this approach scales linearly with N, and in latency-sensitive settings the tail (p95) cost matters more than the mean.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: A natural question is whether some candidates can be identified as unlikely to succeed early—before full evaluation—and skipped, reducing both latency and the number of expansions (model evaluations) without sacrificing accuracy.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: This project investigates a complementary approach: a *negative-exit controller* that operates at the candidate level in a search trace, learning to predict which candidates will fail and exiting them before full evaluation.

evidence_refs.quote: # Real-Model Negative-Exit Controller on Math and Code Search Traces

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: The central hypothesis is that explicit negative-exit labels allow the controller to prune low-value candidates more effectively than a positive-only baseline (which evaluates candidates in order and stops at the first correct one), yielding reductions in p95 latency and p95 expansion count at matched task accuracy.
